name: Glastonbury Festival
tagline: Five days on Worthy Farm where 200,000 people build, and then dissolve, the greatest temporary city on Earth.
dates: June 23–27, 2027 (confirmed; 2026 is a fallow year — no festival)
history: Glastonbury began on 19 September 1970, the day after Jimi Hendrix died, when dairy farmer Michael Eavis invited around 1,500 people to Worthy Farm for the 'Pilton Pop, Blues & Folk Festival'. Admission was £1 and included free milk from the farm; T. Rex headlined after The Kinks pulled out. The 1971 edition — held at the summer solstice, free of charge, and featuring the first Pyramid Stage built on a ley line above the Glastonbury Abbey/Stonehenge axis — set the festival's mystic, countercultural tone. Through the 1980s it grew in partnership with CND (the peace movement's logo still echoes through the site), and the Pyramid Stage became one of the most recognisable silhouettes in live music.

Today Glastonbury is the largest greenfield festival in the world: some 210,000 people, over 100 stages and areas — Pyramid, Other Stage, West Holts, The Park, the after-dark sprawl of Shangri-La and Block9, the Healing Fields, a circus, a cinema, and Arcadia's flame-throwing spider. It is a five-day city with its own newspaper, water network and railway line (the old track bed now forms a main artery). Roughly every five years the farm takes a 'fallow year' to let the land and the village of Pilton recover — 2026 is one, which makes 2027 the eagerly awaited return and the reason demand will be even more ferocious than usual.

Its cultural moments are British folklore: David Bowie at dawn in 1971 and his triumphant 2000 return; the mud baths of 1997 and 2005; Jay-Z silencing the 'no hip-hop at Glasto' debate in 2008 by opening with 'Wonderwall'; Dolly Parton drawing one of the biggest crowds ever seen in 2014; Stormzy's era-defining 2019 headline set; Elton John's UK farewell in 2023. Since 1981 the festival has donated millions to Oxfam, Greenpeace and WaterAid, and Worthy Farm returns to grazing cows within weeks of the last encore.
whyGo: There is no event on Earth like it — a temporary city the size of Bern where the world's biggest acts share billing with cabaret, rave culture, political debate and 3 a.m. weirdness in the South East Corner. Because 2026 is a fallow year, the 2027 edition is the first Glastonbury in two years, and the pilgrimage feeling will be at its strongest. If you get a ticket, you go; the ticket is the hard part, not the decision.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Bristol (BRS) direct with easyJet, then National Express/Bakers Dolphin festival coach or train Bristol Temple Meads → Castle Cary + free shuttle bus to the site
gettingThere.notes: Bristol is the closest airport (~35 km). Book the flight the moment you win tickets in November — festival week fares spike. The official coach+ticket packages from Bristol sell in the same sale.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → London Heathrow/Gatwick (SWISS/BA/easyJet), then GWR train London Paddington → Castle Cary (~1h30) and the free festival shuttle to Pedestrian Gate A
gettingThere.notes: More flight choice than Bristol. Book GWR advance fares ~12 weeks out; Castle Cary shuttle queues are longest Wednesday morning — aim for an early Paddington departure.

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Paris Gare de Lyon (TGV Lyria, ~4h05), cross to Gare du Nord, Eurostar → London St Pancras (~2h20), then Paddington → Castle Cary + shuttle
gettingThere.notes: The flight-free option — doable in a day if you leave Zürich before 07:00. Book Eurostar and Lyria ~4 months ahead; break the trip in Paris on the way home.

costs.notes: The ticket itself dominates: expect roughly £400 + fees for 2027 (2025 was £378.50 + £5), paid as a £75 deposit in November with the balance in April. Thrifty = easyJet to Bristol, bring-your-own tent, festival food. Midrange = flexible flights, pre-pitched Worthy View tent or Sticklinch, a pub night in Wells either side. Splurge = boutique 'glamping' just outside the fence (Pop-Up Hotel etc., often £2,000+ alone) plus hospitality transfers.

booking.label: Registration & tickets (See Tickets — registration is free and mandatory)

weather: Late-June Somerset is a coin flip: 15–22°C days, cool 8–12°C nights, and a real chance of either a heatwave or the legendary mud. Pack for both — wellies or waterproof boots, a proper rain shell, sunscreen and a hat. A cheap foam mat for sitting on damp grass earns its space in the pack.
tips: Register NOW (free, needs a passport-style photo) at glastonbury.seetickets.com — registration is open for 2027 and you cannot buy a ticket without it. Every person in your group must be registered before the November 2026 sale.

The ticket sale (coach packages late Oct / general early-to-mid Nov 2026) sells out in under an hour. Form a group of up to six, share registration numbers in advance, and have everyone try simultaneously — one person through can book for six.
Missed out? The resale of returned deposit tickets happens in April 2027 and is your genuine second chance — same drill, faster sellout.
Arrive Wednesday morning when gates open: you'll get a decent camping spot (Pennard Hill for atmosphere, further out for sleep) and two music-free days to learn the site before the crowds peak.

Bring a folding trolley or rucksack you can carry 2+ km — the walk from car parks/shuttle drop to your pitch is long. No glass on site; card and contactless are accepted everywhere but queues are shorter with some cash.
Don't try to see everything. Pick one must-see per day, then wander — the South East Corner (Shangri-La, Block9) after midnight and a sunrise at the Stone Circle are the real Glastonbury.

localRecs.name: Glastonbury Tor
localRecs.note: The mystical hill above the Somerset Levels, crowned by St Michael's Tower — you can see the festival site from the top. Climb it on your way in or out; it's a 20-minute walk from Glastonbury town.

localRecs.name: Wells Cathedral
localRecs.note: England's smallest city is 15 minutes from Pilton, and its 13th-century cathedral with the scissor arches is a stunning decompression stop before flying home.

localRecs.name: Queen of Cups, Glastonbury
localRecs.note: Modern Middle Eastern-leaning cooking in Glastonbury town — one of Somerset's best restaurants and the antidote to five days of festival food. Book ahead for festival week.

localRecs.name: Roth Bar & Grill, Hauser & Wirth Somerset (Bruton)
localRecs.note: World-class art gallery on a restored farm 20 minutes east, with a superb farm-to-table restaurant — a perfect post-festival recovery lunch near Castle Cary station.

localRecs.name: Burns the Bread, Glastonbury
localRecs.note: Beloved local bakery — grab pasties and lardy cake for the walk-in or the journey home.
